data.title: AT&T (T) Is About to Report Q4 Earnings. Options Traders Are Bracing for a 4.58% Move in the Telecom Stock
data.description:
Telecom giant AT&T (T) is scheduled to announce its results for the fourth quarter of 2025 on January 28. While there are concerns about stiff competition and higher debt levels, several analysts are optimistic about AT&T due to the demand for bundled 5G wireless and fiber broadband plans and initiatives to bolster its network, including a $23 billion deal to acquire wireless spectrum licenses from EchoStar (SATS). According to TipRanks' Options Tool, options traders expect about a 4.58% move in either direction in AT&T stock in reaction to Q4 2025 results.

This implied move is higher than AT&T stock's average post-earnings move (in absolute terms) of 2.56% over the past four quarters.

data.title: 'GOOGL Could Hit This Level Soon': Josh Beck Sets Target Ahead of Earnings
data.description:
Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL) has spent the past year transforming itself from an AI laggard into an AI leader, with only Nvidia standing in the way of its claim to the world's most valuable company.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Indeed, investors not long ago feared that generative AI would undermine Google Search itself. Today, that same technology is viewed as a growth engine, putting Alphabet in a strong position to capitalize on the AI wave rather than fall victim to it.

In fact, Raymond James analyst Josh Beck believes Alphabet's improving AI credentials are strong enough to warrant a rethink of his GOOGL model.

"While we were constructive on checks/DOJ potential last year, our updated bottom-up analysis of GCP (GPU, TPU, PaaS) and Search (Core, AIO, AIM, Gemini) leads to a material upward revision in 2026/27 estimates with RJ sitting at the Street high for 2027 revenue," Beck, who ranks among the top 4% of Street stock experts, said.

As such, Beck has now not only upgraded his GOOGL rating from Outperform (i.e., Buy) to Strong Buy, but has also raised his price target from $315 to a Street-high of $400. Should that figure be met, investors will be sitting on gains of 22% a year from now. (To watch Beck's track record, click here)

The 5-star analyst thinks the company is likely at the start of a period marked by a strengthening AI stack narrative and upward estimate revisions that might "create one of the highest quality top-line AI acceleration stories in the public universe." For 2026, Beck's "baseline assumption" is that performance across mega-cap Internet stocks will be driven primarily by improvements in the AI stack narrative and underlying fundamentals, rather than by a "mean reversion trade" focused on buying compressed multiples and selling elevated ones.

As for the numbers, Beck is calling for GCP growth of 44% in 2026 and 36% in 2027, compared with Street expectations of 34% and 31%, respectively. This outlook is based on approximately 2 million TPUs generating around $25 billion in ARR, roughly 0.75 million GPUs contributing about $20 billion in ARR, Gemini API PaaS at approximately $10 billion in ARR, and Vertex PaaS at roughly $2.5 billion in ARR by the end of 2027. While each 1 million TPU units sold at $20,000 could represent a $20 billion revenue opportunity, hardware sales are not included in the model at this stage, as Beck believes further progress is needed in the low-level software stack, such as TorchTPU.

In Search, Beck forecasts growth of 13% in both 2026 and 2027, vs. Street estimates of 11% and 9%. This assumes declines in core search of 5% to 10% are more than offset by the scaling of AIO, AIM, and Gemini to 4 billion, 2 billion, and 1 billion daily active users, respectively, by the end of 2027, along with high-single-digit CPC (cost per click) growth driven by a rising mix of AI-powered search queries that improve context and conversion.

In a bull case, Beck thinks U.S. AI Assistant ARPU could reach $750, as more complex, context-rich queries drive a "structural improvement" in CTR (click-through rate) and CVR (conversion rate) and establish a higher ceiling for CPCs. For Google, Beck expects AI Assistants to be "diffused across various surfaces," primarily AIO, followed by AIM and Gemini.

That's Beck's view, but how does the rest of Wall Street stack up? The Raymond James analyst now joins 25 others in the bullish camp on GOOGL, while 7 Hold ratings aren't enough to shake the overall verdict: a Strong Buy consensus rating. (See GOOGL stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Microsoft Stock (MSFT) Gets Multiple Price Target Cuts on Software Concerns
data.description:
Several analysts have lowered their price targets on Microsoft (MSFT) stock, citing growing concerns about software sector valuation compression.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Analysts at Cantor Fitzgerald reiterated a Buy-equivalent overweight rating on MSFT stock but reduced their price target on the shares to $590 from $639. Swiss bank UBS (UBS) cut its target on MSFT stock to $600 from $650, while maintaining a Buy rating.

Both Cantor Fitzgerald and UBS expect another strong quarter from Microsoft when it reports its financial results on Jan. 28. However, the price target cuts reflect valuation concerns across the software space. The price target cuts come with MSFT stock down 7% so far this year.

AI's Impact on Software

Microsoft isn't alone in seeing its share price slump. Software stocks have been badly beaten down over the last six months as concerns rise about the impact of artificial intelligence (AI) on the sector and the future of the industry.

Cantor Fitzgerald stresses that stock price multiples are compressing in software and notes that Microsoft isn't immune to those pressures. UBS has a similar view and says that Wall Street's current expectations for the company's capital expenditures are too high. "Given the evident de-rating across the software sector, we're trimming our PT from $650 to $600," wrote UBS.

However, not everyone is cutting their price targets on MSFT stock. Morgan Stanley (MS) reiterated an overweight rating and $650 price target on the shares.

Is MSFT Stock a Buy?

Microsoft's stock has a consensus Strong Buy rating among 34 Wall Street analysts. That rating is based on 32 Buy and two Hold recommendations issued in the last three months. The average price target on MSFT stock of $626.14 implies 33.04% upside from current levels.
data.author: Joel Baglole

data.title: 'A Hard Game to Play,' Says Top Investor About Dogecoin (DOGE)
data.description:
Dogecoin (DOGE-USD) might have started life as a joke, but via its strong community, the token has become a powerhouse of the crypto space. This OG meme coin has a market cap of $21.05 billion, although it should be noted that even with that sky-high valuation, DOGE has known better times – the token currently sits 83% below its 2021 high.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

So, asks top investor Neil Patel, is this a "buy-the-dip opportunity that could work out well for investors over the next five years? Or is Dogecoin better left out of your portfolio?"

As noted, Dogecoin benefits from a dedicated community. Launched all the way back in 2013, it is one of the more established names in the crypto space. Its longevity has helped it build a base of supporters who are invested in its success.

On X, Dogecoin's official account commands around 4.3 million followers – more than Ethereum's roughly 4 million, even though Ethereum's market cap is about 16 times larger. Bitcoin, the undisputed heavyweight of crypto, has 8.2 million followers, yet its $1.8 trillion valuation is roughly 84 times bigger than Dogecoin's. To Patel, this imbalance shows that Dogecoin is "punching well above its weight" on social media compared with its actual size in the market.

"If nothing else," Patel explains, "these followers can provide a floor for Dogecoin's price because there will always be interest. This means that five years from now, it won't be completely worthless."

Still, Patel – who ranks among the top 1% of investors on TipRanks – tempers that optimism with a reality check: there's no reliable way to predict how strong that community support will remain over time. In the end, Dogecoin's future may depend less on technology and more on whether its crowd of believers keeps showing up.

Essentially, Dogecoin's utility is fairly limited. For investors interested in blockchains that support smart contracts, platforms like Ethereum (ETH-USD), Solana (SOL-USD), and Cardano (ADA-USD) may offer more potential than Dogecoin. Its network is relatively simple, and there are only a small number of developers actively working to expand its capabilities.

That said, some projects are underway. GigaWallet, for instance, enables businesses to accept Dogecoin payments more easily, and DogeOS aims to serve as a development layer on top of Dogecoin to support decentralized applications. While these initiatives could, in theory, increase adoption and demand, Patel notes that other cryptocurrencies are currently further ahead in terms of development and innovation.

In the meantime, it does look like market interest in Dogecoin is waning. The cryptocurrency is currently trading 83% below its all-time high, and it dropped 61% over 2025. Its limited utility only adds to the challenges it faces. And while Dogecoin can see short-term price spikes, these gains have historically been short-lived, contributing to a "highly volatile environment" that many investors might prefer to steer clear of.

"Looking out to early 2031, I wouldn't be surprised at all if Dogecoin was worth less than what it is today," the 5-star investor summed up. (To watch Patel's track record, click here)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: The Week That Was, The Week Ahead: Macro and Markets, Jan. 25
data.description:
U.S. stocks finished the week mixed as gains in technology shares helped offset weakness in financial stocks and rising rate pressure. The Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) fell 0.58% to $49,098.71, the S&P 500 (SPX) ended nearly flat, up 0.03% to $6,915.61, and the Nasdaq 100 (NDX) outperformed, rising 0.34% to $25,605.47.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Meanwhile, interest rates continued to move higher. The 10-year Treasury yield climbed to about 4.23%, reflecting steady inflation concerns and caution ahead of earnings. In commodities, oil (CL) gained 2.78% to $61.24 per barrel as energy demand expectations firmed, while gold (XAUUSD) rose 0.7% to $4,982.50 as investors balanced risk exposure. Bitcoin (BTC) slipped 0.61% to $89,551 as digital assets paused after recent gains.
data.author: Ran Melamed

data.title: 'Stay Away': Investors Sound the Alarm on XRP
data.description:
XRP (XRP-USD) was one of the biggest performers in the crypto rally that followed the 2024 U.S. election, surging more than 540% over roughly six weeks as investors bet on a friendlier regulatory climate and a potential resolution of the SEC's long-running lawsuit against Ripple over alleged unregistered securities sales.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Indeed, those catalysts played out last year, but since the court case was settled, XRP has given back a big chunk of those gains and now sits 48% below last year's highs.

For those hoping the depressed price action signals opportunity, some investors have recently been quick to pour cold water on that notion.

For example, investor Emma Newbery points to two glaring issues with the investment case.

One revolves around the tokenomics. When Ripple launched XRP, it created 100 billion tokens, locking about 34 billion in escrow. With 66 billion circulating, Ripple holds enough to influence the price. Moreover, unlike Bitcoin, which is capped at just $21 million coins, scarcity is unlikely to drive value.

Secondly, Newbery believes XRP's "use case is questionable." Ripple seems to be moving away from its original goal of providing low-cost cross-border payments. CEO Brad Garlinghouse recently told CNBC that the company is acquiring traditional finance firms to integrate its crypto solutions. While this may be a smart strategy for Ripple, it does little for XRP itself.

XRP's main role is as a bridge currency in Ripple's payment network, but that position is increasingly under pressure. Swift, the long-standing international payments network, is developing its own blockchain-based solutions, and other financial firms are exploring similar technologies. At the same time, stablecoins allow fast, low-cost international transfers without the price volatility of XRP, making the token less essential.

As such, Newbery thinks the outlook for XRP is "not as rosy as bulls might suggest."

Also homing in on the use case issue, investor Johnny Rice thinks there's an "important distinction investors need to understand between XRP itself and related products."

When people discuss adoption, says Rice, they're usually referring to the uptake of Ripple's financial products, not XRP itself. Banks can use these services without holding – or even interacting with – XRP.

RippleNet, the company's flagship product, illustrates this. Essentially a messaging platform, it competes with the traditional SWIFT system by enabling faster, cheaper settlements. While XRP can be used within RippleNet, most banks stick to fiat currencies, taking advantage of the network's technology rather than the token.

At the same time, even after the big pullback, Rice thinks XRP's valuation is hard to justify. Unlike stocks, cryptocurrencies don't have traditional metrics like earnings, cash flow, or assets to analyze.

One imperfect – but telling – comparison is Visa. The payment network has a ~$622 billion market cap, less than 5.5 times XRP's $116 billion, yet it handles roughly 640 times more transactions. While it's not a direct "apples-to-apples comparison," it highlights the stark gap between XRP's market value and the scale of actual transaction activity.

"At the end of the day," Rice summed up, "I believe XRP's current value is built on hype and speculation. That means it could reverse course and spike above $2.50 or higher in the near term, but it won't hold that value over the long term, and I would not own XRP." (To watch Rice's track record, click here)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Meta or Netflix: Cathie Wood Cuts Back on One Top Tech Stock While Doubling Down on Another
data.description:
Whether you're a fan of her investing style or not, there's no denying Cathie Wood does things her own way. The Ark Invest CEO tends to make big, high-conviction bets on disruptive technologies and is far more comfortable with volatility than most traditional fund managers. That focus on bold ideas is what makes her polarizing – Wood tends to draw plenty of criticism when markets turn against her, and renewed enthusiasm when those same themes come back into favor.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Wood's predilection for eye-catching predictions was on display again this week. In Ark Invest's Big Ideas 2026 report, the firm forecasts that Bitcoin's market cap will rise to $16 trillion by 2030, suggesting each coin will be worth roughly $800,000, an ~800% jump from current levels.

As for the more day-to-day matter of portfolio management, Wood recently made moves involving two recent underperformers. Both Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) and Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) have struggled in recent months, but it appears that Wood thinks one of these names is worth considering at the moment, while the other has been receiving the trimming treatment. 

So, we've given these market giants a closer look to find out why one seems more appealing to Wood than the other right now. Let's dive in.

Meta Platforms

First up, Meta Platforms, the social media behemoth owner of Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp. With billions of users across its family of apps and products that are deeply embedded in daily life, the company is known worldwide. Its scale, reach, and data footprint have long made it one of the most influential companies on the global scene, with the ability to even influence election outcomes. Additionally, it is an expert in monetizing attention through targeted advertising, which is still a core element of its business.

But not all Meta touches turn to gold. Famously, the Mark Zuckerberg-led company went all in on the metaverse, once considered the future of the internet, rebranding the company in the process and spending billions on virtual-reality research and the development of hardware, software, and 3D virtual environments. However, that failed to catch on, and the company decided to turn its attention in recent years to where all tech roads lead – AI.

But here, at the moment at least, Meta also seems to be running into problems. At present, Meta is widely seen as trailing rivals such as OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic in the AI race, despite spending heavily. Much of that perception comes from the fact Meta has yet to produce an AI product that clearly sets it apart and delivers real profitability, while it has a tendency to move fast and address problems later, a strategy that has resulted in quality issues.

Maybe Wood is waiting for Meta to get its AI game sorted before getting bullish again. This month, via the ARKF, ARKK, and ARKW funds, she sold $20.8 million worth of META shares.

Needham's Laura Martin echoes that caution. The analyst believes the company's AI struggles are likely to continue, posing a problem for shareholders.

"Meta investors seem to have growing investment risks owing to its small size, strategy clutter, and lack of incremental revenue streams to offset a portion of its GenAI capital spending. We believe META's risk/reward for public shareholders is deteriorating as Meta uses 100% of its FCF plus takes on debt and off-balance sheet obligations, without a clear path to positive returns on growing levels of capital intensity," Martin explained. "The GenAI battle is among giants, and META's size and debt capacity are far smaller than GOOGL and AMZN. To fund its CapX investments, press reports say META is structuring off-balance sheet liabilities via SPVs, which are senior obligations to equity value and, therefore, represent a headwind to share price appreciation, we believe."

Accordingly, Martin rates META shares a Hold (i.e., Neutral) without having a fixed price target in mind. (To watch Martin's track record, click here)

5 other analysts share that neutral view. On the bearish end of the spectrum, one analyst remains negative, but that lone Sell is heavily outnumbered by 37 Buy ratings. Altogether, this mix translates into a Strong Buy consensus rating. Based on the Street's average price target of $820.21, Meta is projected to deliver 24.5% upside over the next 12 months. (See Meta stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel
